title: The Ultimate Capital Gains Rental Property Canada Landlord Guide
metaDescription: Capital gains rental property Canada landlord guide: Learn CRA rules and regulations to minimize tax burden
content:
As a busy landlord with a full-time day job, tracking capital gains and rental income can be a daunting task. The capital gains rental property Canada landlord guide is here to help you navigate the complex world of tax regulations and ensure you're taking advantage of all the deductions available to you. In this guide, we'll cover the ins and outs of capital gains and rental income, including how to calculate your gains, what expenses are eligible for deduction, and how to report your income on your tax return.

## Introduction to Capital Gains
Capital gains are profits made from the sale of a property, and they're subject to taxation. As a landlord, you'll need to calculate your capital gains when you sell a rental property. The capital gains rental property Canada landlord guide will walk you through the process, including how to determine your adjusted cost base (ACB) and how to calculate your capital gains. For example, let's say you purchased a rental property for $200,000 and sold it for $300,000. Your capital gain would be $100,000, which would be subject to taxation.

## Calculating Capital Gains
To calculate your capital gains, you'll need to determine your ACB, which includes the original purchase price of the property, plus any improvements or renovations made to the property. You'll also need to subtract any depreciation or capital cost allowance (CCA) claimed on the property. For instance, if you claimed $10,000 in CCA on the property, your ACB would be $190,000. You can find more information on calculating your ACB in our guide to [CRA-Compliant Bookkeeping for Canadian Landlords: The Complete Guide](https://www.rentalops.ca/blog/cra-compliant-bookkeeping-canadian-landlords-complete-guide).

## Reporting Rental Income
In addition to capital gains, you'll also need to report your rental income on your tax return. This includes rent received, as well as any other income related to the property, such as parking or laundry fees. You'll need to complete a T776 statement, which will help you calculate your net rental income. For example, let's say you received $20,000 in rent and had $5,000 in expenses related to the property. Your net rental income would be $15,000, which would be reported on Line 160 of your tax return.

## Eligible Expenses
As a landlord, you're eligible to deduct a wide range of expenses related to your rental property. These include mortgage interest (Line 8710), property taxes (Line 8420), insurance (Line 8720), maintenance and repairs (Line 8960), and advertising (Line 8520). You can also deduct the cost of any materials or supplies used to maintain or repair the property. For instance, if you spent $1,000 on materials to repair a leaky roof, you could deduct that amount on Line 8960.

## Common Mistakes Small Landlords Make
One of the most common mistakes small landlords make is failing to keep accurate records of their expenses. This can lead to missed deductions and a higher tax bill. Another mistake is misclassifying expenses or failing to claim depreciation or CCA. For example, if you claimed $5,000 in expenses related to the property, but $1,000 of that was for personal use, you could be audited and forced to pay back the excess claim. You can avoid these mistakes by keeping accurate records and consulting with a tax professional.

Some other common mistakes include:
* Missing structural depreciation or misclassifying line items
* Failing to claim capital cost allowance (CCA) on eligible assets
* Not keeping accurate records of rent received and expenses paid

## What This Costs You Without the Right Tools
Without the right tools, tracking your rental income and expenses can be a time-consuming and costly process. You could spend 2-3 hours per month tracking receipts and expenses manually, which could cost you $100-$200 per month in lost productivity. Alternatively, you could use a software like RentalOps, which would cost $6.99 per month and eliminate the need for manual data entry. You can learn more about the benefits of using RentalOps in our guide to [Reporting Rental Income in Canada: A Guide for Part-Year Landlords](https://www.rentalops.ca/blog/reporting-rental-income-in-canada-a-guide-for-part-year-landlords).

## Key Takeaways
Here are the key takeaways from this guide:
* Capital gains are profits made from the sale of a property and are subject to taxation
* You'll need to calculate your adjusted cost base (ACB) to determine your capital gain
* You can deduct a wide range of expenses related to your rental property, including mortgage interest, property taxes, and maintenance and repairs
* Keeping accurate records is crucial to avoiding mistakes and ensuring you're taking advantage of all eligible deductions
* Using a software like RentalOps can save you time and money by eliminating the need for manual data entry

If you're looking for more information on converting a rental property to personal use, check out our guide to [Converting Rental Property to Personal Use in Canada: A Step-by-Step Guide](https://www.rentalops.ca/blog/converting-rental-property-to-personal-use-in-canada-a-step-by-step-guide).

To get started with tracking your rental income and expenses, download our Free T776 Landlord Excel Template at [https://www.rentalops.ca/free-landlord-t776-excel-template](https://www.rentalops.ca/free-landlord-t776-excel-template). This template will help you set up your record-keeping perfectly for your accountant and make it easy to track your expenses and income throughout the year. Once you have your records in order, you can seamlessly transition to RentalOps automatic tracking software to eliminate data entry entirely.
